pathophysiology_summary: A vesiculobullous ('inflammatory') tinea pedis is a brisk hypersensitivity (id-type) reaction to a dermatophyte, triggered by occlusion and sweating, producing crops of tense, deep-seated clear vesicles/bullae on an erythematous base over the insteps and plantar vaults, intensely itchy/burning. The concurrent interdigital maceration/peeling, moccasin scaling and nail change confirm the underlying dermatophyte. Discriminators: the concurrent classic tinea (web-space maceration, moccasin scaling, onychomycosis) points to a dermatophyte trigger rather than primary dyshidrotic eczema (pompholyx); the clear serous (not sterile yellow pustular) fluid argues against palmoplantar pustulosis; and the deep-seated tense (not flaccid, easily rupturing, honey-crusted) vesicles argue against bullous impetigo. Management is a SHORT course of a potent topical corticosteroid to the blistering areas to rapidly settle the severe inflammation, PLUS a topical antifungal (terbinafine) to the whole foot for ~4 weeks to treat the cause, airing/drying the feet, footwear/sock measures, and not popping the blisters (the roof protects against infection). Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Tense clear plantar vesicles WITH web-space maceration/moccasin scaling/nail change.
Similar vesicles but without concurrent classic tinea features.
Sterile yellow pustules, not clear serous vesicles.
Flaccid easily-rupturing honey-crusted bullae, not deep tense pruritic vesicles.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise vesiculobullous tinea (from concurrent classic tinea features); short potent steroid for the blistering + long antifungal; don't pop blisters.

differential.evidence_basis: Clear tense vesicles + classic tinea; not pustular/impetiginous; no secondary infection.
Fluid is clear serous, not sterile yellow pustules.
Working diagnosis: short potent topical steroid + whole-foot antifungal + airing/drying; don't pop blisters; infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (blisters turning to thick yellow pus, hot spreading ankle redness, fever)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating as pure pompholyx/eczema and not treating the underlying tinea, causing relapse.
Advising draining the blisters, exposing raw skin to infection.
Suppressing inflammation without treating the fungal cause.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Short potent topical steroid to blistering areas + whole-foot antifungal + airing/drying/footwear measures; don't pop blisters; review/urgent care if secondary infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing the fungal cause, or popping blisters/causing infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Inflammatory (vesiculobullous) tinea pedis: a short potent topical corticosteroid settles the acute reaction while a topical (± oral) antifungal treats the dermatophyte; keep feet dry; treat footwear.
